1: The transition formula wayyiškab Yehōšāpāṭ ʿim-ʾăḇōtāyw (‘Jehoshaphat lay down with his ancestors’) is the standard regnal conclusion formula (cf. 1 Kings 11:43; 2 Chronicles 14:20; 2 Chronicles 15:8). Burial in the ʿîr Dāwîd (city of David) was the honorific resting place for Judah’s faithful kings; its mention here contrasts with Jehoram’s exclusion from the royal tombs in v20. The succession formula wayyimlōḵ ʾaḥăzYāhû bᵉnô taḥtāyw (‘his son Ahaziah reigned in his place’) is formulaic but the Chronicler uses the correct dynastic name here (cf. 2 Chronicles 22).
2: Jehoshaphat’s sons are listed with two sons named ‘Azariah’ — a duplication the Chronicler preserves without harmonization. This is characteristic of Hebrew name-lists where family naming patterns led to identical names in the same generation. The list closes with the explanatory phrase kōl-ʾēlleh bᵉnê Yehōšāpāṭ melek Yiśrāʾēl (‘all these are sons of Jehoshaphat king of Israel’), using Yiśrāʾēl rather than Yᵉhûdāh — the Chronicler’s inclusive usage for the whole covenant people under the Davidic king.
3: Mattānôt rabbôt (‘generous gifts’) — the plural of mattānāh (gift, given thing; H4976) reflects the ANE practice of providing for secondary sons to forestall succession conflict. ʿārê mibṣār (‘fortified cities’) given to the brothers created regional power bases outside Jerusalem. The causal kî hûʾ habbᵉḵôr (‘for he was the firstborn’) — bᵉḵôr (H1060) carries ANE legal weight as the heir to both the doubled inheritance and, in Judah’s case, the dynastic succession.
4: wayyitḥazzaq Yᵉhôrām (‘Jehoram established himself’) — the Hithpael of ḥāzaq (H2388) carries a reflexive-assertive sense: he strengthened himself into position, seized power. The verb is frequently used for military or political consolidation. wayya-harōg ʾet-kol-ʾeḥāyw beḥāreḇ (‘he put all his brothers to the sword’) — hārag (H2026): to kill, slay. The phrase û-miśśārê Yiśrāʾēl (‘and some of the Israelite leaders’) is unique to Chronicles, indicating purge beyond the family.
5: The accession formula ben-šelōšîm ûšetayim šānāh Yᵉhôrām bᵉmalᵉkô follows the standard pattern: age at accession + regnal length. Jehoram’s eight-year reign (eight being the most frequent number for short, uninspiring reigns in the Chronicler’s framework) combines with his age (thirty-two) to mark him as relatively young at accession yet without the energy of genuine reform. The Chronicler notes Jerusalem without the usual ûšᵉmô ʾimmô (‘and his mother’s name was’) formula, a notable omission.
6: The evaluation formula wayyēlek bᵉdereḵ malkê Yiśrāʾēl (‘he followed the way of the kings of Israel’) — dereḵ (H1870): way, road, manner — is the Chronicler’s behavioral shorthand for Baal-worship patterns. The reason given: kî bat-ʾAḥʾāḇ hāyᵉtāh lô lᵉʾiššāh (‘because the daughter of Ahab was his wife’). The dynastic marriage to the Omride house is the theological-historical explanation for Baal influence in Jerusalem. The standard condemnation wayyaʿaś hāraʿ bᵉʿênê YHWH follows.
7: nîr (H5216) — the ‘lamp’ or ‘light’ idiom for Davidic dynastic continuation: YHWH’s promise is to provide nîr lô ûlᵉḇānāyw kol-hayyāmîm (‘a lamp for him and his sons for all days’). The image of a lamp that must not go out reflects the threat of dynastic extinction that runs through 2 Chronicles 21–24, culminating in the Joash rescue (2 Chronicles 22:10-12). The Chronicler specifies that YHWH made a bᵉrît (covenant) with David — the Davidic covenant is the theological brake on Jehoram’s destructive reign. Without it, the dynasty would have been destroyed for its wickedness.
8: pāšaʿ ʾĕdôm mittaḥat yad-Yᵉhûdāh (‘Edom rebelled against Judah’s authority’) — pāšaʿ (H6586): to rebel, transgress, break away. Edom’s subjection to Judah had been established under David (2 Samuel 8:14) and Jehoshaphat maintained it. The rebellion is both political and the Chronicler’s theological sign of YHWH withdrawing protection from Jehoram’s kingdom. wayyamlîḵû ʿălêhem melek (‘and crowned their own king’) — they installed their own king against Judah’s suzerainty.
9: wayyaʿăḇōr Yᵉhôrām ʿim-śārāyw (‘Jehoram crossed over with his commanders’) — ʿāḇar (H5674) with military connotations: to cross over, march through. The phrase wayyāqom laylāh wayyak ʾet-ʾĔdôm (‘he got up at night and broke through’) — the night assault; nākāh (H5221): to smite, strike. The parallel in 2 Kings 8:21 is similar. The Chronicler’s ‘when the Edomites surrounded him and his chariot commanders’ clarifies that Jehoram barely escaped; this is not a victory but an escape from encirclement.
10: wayyipšaʿ ʾĔdôm mittaḥat yad-Yᵉhûdāh ʿad hayyôm hazzeh (‘Edom has remained independent to this day’) — the historian’s perspective; ʿad hayyôm hazzeh is a common formulaic anchor to the time of writing, confirming the permanent loss. The coordinate clause ʾāz tipšaʿ Libnāh (‘at the same time Libnah rebelled’) — a southwestern city on the Philistine border. The causal particle kî ʿāzaḇ ʾet-YHWH (‘because he abandoned the LORD’) — ʿāzaḇ (H5800): to abandon, leave; the Chronicler’s key apostasy verb.
11: bāmôt (H1116) — the high places: unauthorized worship sites condemned throughout Kings/Chronicles as violations of centralized cult at Jerusalem. wayyizᵉneh ʾet-yōšᵉḇê Yᵉrûšālayim (‘seduced the people of Jerusalem into spiritual prostitution’) — zānāh (H2181, Hiphil): to cause to play the whore; the idiom of spiritual unfaithfulness used for idolatry throughout the Prophets (Hosea 1:2; Jeremiah 2:20). MKT renders ‘spiritual prostitution’ to capture the metaphorical force of the original without a literal rendering that would confuse English readers about the actual referent.
12: miktāb (H4385) — a letter, written document; the term appears only here and Jeremiah 36:6 in the Hebrew Bible for a prophetic communication in written form. This is the only letter Elijah is said to have written. The chronological puzzle: Elijah was taken up to heaven before Jehoram’s Judean reign (cf. 2 Kgs 2). Rabbinic tradition resolved this by positing Elijah wrote the letter before his translation and it was delivered later, or that the reference is to an earlier letter to Ahab’s dynasty more broadly. The Chronicler uses the letter as a direct prophetic address to Jehoram regardless of this historical puzzle.
13: ûbᵉnê ʾāḇîḵā hatôḇîm mimmeḵā haragtā (‘you have murdered your brothers, your own family, who were better men than you’) — the phrase hatôḇîm mimmeḵā (‘better than you’) is a rare explicit comparative moral judgment within a prophetic oracle. The comparison inverts the usual evaluation: the king who survived is morally inferior to those he killed. wayyazᵉneh ʾet-Yᵉhûdāh wᵉʾet-yōšᵉḇê Yᵉrûšālayim — the verb repeated from v11, forming a bracket around the prophetic indictment.
14: maggēpāh gᵉdôlāh (‘devastating blow’) — maggēpāh (H4046): a blow, plague, striking; often used for divine punishment through epidemic or military defeat. The sequence of objects struck — ʿamᵉḵā ûḇānêḵā ûnāšêḵā wᵉḵol-rᵉkûšeḵā (people, children, wives, all possessions) — follows the ascending order of personal loss, with the attack on family as the most devastating consequence. This fulfillment is narrated in v16-17.
15: ḥŏlî məʿêḵā (‘bowel disease’) — maʿeh (H4578): the entrails, bowels; the disease produces progressive intestinal deterioration. The phrase ʿad-ṣēʾt māʿêḵā min-haḥŏlî yāmîm ʿal-yāmîm (‘until your intestines come out because of the illness, day by day’) is graphically clinical for prophetic literature, emphasizing the gradual, publicly visible nature of divine judgment. The fulfillment at v18-19 confirms the letter’s authority.
16: ûbaʿĔlîšāʾ ʿōrēr YHWH ʿal-Yᵉhôrām (‘the LORD stirred up against Jehoram’) — ʿûr (H5782, Hiphil): to arouse, wake up, stir; YHWH as the agent who mobilizes foreign armies against his disobedient people (cf. Isaiah 10:5; Jeremiah 51:11). haPᵉlištîm wᵉhāʿărāḇîm (Philistines and Arabians) — the Arabians (ʿărāḇîm, H6163) here are the nomadic peoples bordering the Negev/Sinai region. ʿal-yad haKûšîm (‘who bordered the Cushites’) — adjacent to the Cushite regions, possibly meaning Arab tribes in the Ethiopian-border area.
17: wayyišbû ʾet-kol-hāreḵûš (‘carried off everything’) — šāḇāh (H7617): to take captive, lead away; reḵûš (H7399): movable property, possessions, wealth. The royal family is taken: gam-bānāyw gam-nāšāyw (sons and wives). The exception: Yᵉhôʾāḥāz bᵉnô haqqāṭōn (‘Jehoahaz his youngest son was left’). In 2 Kings 8:24 the same person is called Ahaziah; Jehoahaz is an alternative form of the same name (both contain YHWH + ʾāḥaz/ʾāhaz, seize). The Chronicler uses the longer form here only.
18: wayyiggoph YHWH ʾet-Yᵉhôrām ḥŏlî lōʾ lᵉrāpāʾ (‘the LORD afflicted Jehoram with an incurable disease’) — nāgap (H5062): to smite, strike; used for divine blows in plague contexts. lōʾ lᵉrāpāʾ (‘with no healing’) — rāpāʾ (H7495): to heal; the combination ‘without healing’ appears also in Jeremiah 14:19 and Micah 1:9 for irremediable divine judgment. The fulfillment of Elijah’s letter (v15) is exact: the disease is identical to what was promised.
19: bᵉtaḥălûʾîm rāʿîm (‘in severe pain’) — taḥălûʾ (H8463): sickness, disease; the plural intensive suggests overwhelming suffering. wᵉlōʾ ʿāśû-lô ʿammô śᵉrēpāh kišrēpat ʾăḇōtāyw (‘his people did not honor him with a burning of spices as they had done for his ancestors’) — the royal funeral included a ceremonial burning of spices as honor (cf. 16:14 for Asa’s honorable burial). The absence marks royal disgrace: Jehoram died unwept and unhonored, with no public mourning.
20: ûlōʾ-ḥemdāh hālaḵ (‘he passed away, to no one’s regret’) — ḥemdāh (H2532): desirableness, pleasure, delight; the negative prefix creates the idiom ‘without anyone desiring him’ — no one longed for or grieved at his death. The burial formula wayyiqbᵉrûhû bᵉʿîr Dāwîd is followed immediately by the disqualifying clause wᵉlōʾ bᵉqiḇrôt hamᵉlāḵîm (‘but not in the royal tombs’) — exclusion from the dynastic burial ground is the ultimate posthumous dishonor, recalling the exclusion of the wicked from communal burial rights.
